nativeRange.habitat: Egger's Killifish inhabits seasonal ephemeral pools, shallow floodplain swamps, roadside depressions, and marshy ditches on the lowland coastal plains of Tanzania — habitats that dry out completely during the dry season each year.

decorPreferences: fine sand or peat-based substrate for egg-laying
floating plants (water lettuce, frogbit) for surface cover and diffused lighting
spawning mop or small tray of moist peat for egg collection
minimal or no current — still to very slow-moving water
dark substrate to highlight male coloration

diet.notes: Micropredator that strongly prefers small live and frozen meaty foods. Staples include live or frozen Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), chopped bloodworm, and micro-worms. Tubifex and fruit flies also accepted. High-quality small dried foods may be accepted by conditioned fish but live/frozen foods are essential for long-term health and breeding condition. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras in a species-appropriate temperature range
other Nothobranchius species of similar size (species-only tank preferred)
avoidWith: fast or boisterous tankmates that outcompete for food
fish requiring warm tropical temperatures above 26°C
invertebrates that may consume eggs in substrate

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image; confirm availability before use. Shows male coloration.

summary: Egger’s Killifish is a brilliantly coloured annual killifish endemic to Tanzania’s Rufiji basin, available in a blue and a red colour form. Adults stay under 5 cm and live only 6–16 months. Care is considered intermediate, centred on the specialist dry-incubation protocol that eggs require before they can be hatched.
careNotes:
Nothobranchius eggersi is an annual killifish whose wild lifespan is tied to the seasonal drying of its ephemeral pool habitat in coastal Tanzania. In the aquarium the fish rarely exceed 12–16 months even under ideal conditions, so breeders typically maintain rolling egg stocks rather than relying on a single generation. A species-only tank is strongly recommended for breeding: a bare-bottom or shallow peat-substrate vessel of 40–60 L with a tight-fitting lid (the fish are active jumpers) and subdued, diffuse lighting works well. Water should be kept relatively cool (20–26°C); temperatures above 28°C accelerate ageing and reduce reproductive output. Moderately soft to medium-hard, slightly alkaline water matches the brackish-influenced floodplain chemistry of the Rufiji delta.

Two distinct colour forms circulate in the hobby — a blue form (iridescent light-blue body with red-brown scale margins) and a red form (reticulated red pattern over a blue body with vivid caudal fins) — originating from different collection localities across the Rufiji, Ruvu, Wami, and Mligasi drainages. Keep locality strains separate to preserve genetic integrity.

Males can show mild aggression toward one another; the recommended ratio is one male to two or more females. Maintain a spare male in a separate vessel in case the primary male is lost, as females will come into spawning condition regardless. Feed small live or frozen invertebrates two to three times daily; dried foods alone are insufficient for conditioning. The species is range-restricted and subject to habitat loss from agriculture and water extraction, making captive-bred stock preferable to wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the classic annual-killifish protocol. Provide a shallow tray or small container of moist peat (or coconut fibre) as a spawning site within the tank; alternatively a spawning mop laid on fine substrate can be used. Males display vigorously and drive females into the substrate to deposit eggs one at a time. Collect eggs every 1–2 weeks by removing and sifting the peat; rinse eggs gently and inspect under magnification — fertile eggs are round, amber, and slightly opaque. Transfer eggs to a labelled zip-lock bag or sealed container of barely moist peat at room temperature (21–25°C) for a dry-incubation (diapause) period of 3–6 months. Shorter incubation periods (under 3 months) typically yield poor hatch rates; 4–5 months is the most commonly cited optimum. To trigger hatching, flood the peat with slightly soft, aged water at about 24°C. Fry are large enough at hatching to accept newly hatched Artemia nauplii and micro-worms immediately. Sexual maturity is reached in as little as 3–4 weeks, so sex fry early and separate males before aggression escalates. Maintain a cool, stable temperature during incubation; avoid temperatures above 28°C which can kill embryos.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nothobranchius eggersi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size 45–50 mm SL, temperature 20–24°C, pH 7.0–8.2, hardness 179–536 ppm, minimum tank base 45×30 cm, diet (live/frozen Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm), lifespan 6–16 months, native range (lower Rufiji system including Selous Game Reserve and delta), habitat (ephemeral seasonal pools), egg incubation 3–4 months, sexual maturity ~3 weeks.

additional.name: FishBase — Nothobranchius eggersi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.0 cm TL), temperature 24–28°C, native range (lower Rufiji River including delta, eastern Tanzania), taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), habitat (temporary pools and swamps, benthopelagic freshwater), IUCN status Least Concern (LC, 2018), incubation period 2–3 months cited. Temperature range diverges from SeriouslyFish (20–24°C); used SeriouslyFish cooler range as conservative optimum consistent with Aquatic Arts data.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Blue Egger’s Killifish
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 4.5–5 cm, temperature 68–75°F (20–24°C), pH 7.0–8.2, hardness 10–30 dKH, noted blue colour form, escape risk (active jumper), top-water swim zone, compatibility with peaceful fish and invertebrates, species-only for breeding. 15–20 gal minimum for a pair.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Nothobranchius eggersi
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Least Concern status, two colour forms (blue and red), endemic to lower Rufiji basin, Tanzania. Species described 1982 by Seegers; named after German aquarist Gerd Eggers. Common name ‘Orchid Nothobranch’ also in use.

additional.name: killi.co.uk — Nothobranchius eggersi Species Profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist breeder database; confirmed temperature 24–27°C for breeding, peat substrate spawning (100% of breeders), red and blue strains from multiple Tanzanian localities (Rufiji, Ruhoi, Kigongo, etc.), mild intermale aggression noted, live foods strongly preferred. 16 successful breeder reports cited.

fish.breedingNotes: As an annual killifish, Nothobranchius eggersi deposits its eggs into peat or a similar substrate, where they undergo a dry-incubation period — a form of dormancy known as diapause — of 3–6 months before hatching can be triggered by flooding with water. Fry are large enough to accept newly hatched Artemia nauplii immediately at hatching and reach sexual maturity in as little as 3–4 weeks. A species-only breeding tank is recommended, with eggs collected from the spawning substrate every 1–2 weeks.
